data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of December 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the December 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices higher.

Cobalt price news

As of December 18, the cobalt spot price was at US$23.94/lb, higher than US$22.03/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$51,865/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 123 tonnes, slightly down from 126 last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long-term price chart—USD 23.94/lb (source)

Cobalt demand vs. supply forecasts

You can view the latest Trend Investing cobalt demand vs. supply article here.

The new DRC export quota system could potentially flip the ex-DRC market balance from a cobalt surplus to a deficit in 2025 (RHS chart) (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

Cobalt market news

On December 3, Reuters reported:

Congo's attempt to control cobalt market risks becoming supply shock... February's export ban was replaced with a quota system in October but the country's producers are still awaiting the green shipping light from Congo's minerals authority ARECOMS. The withdrawal of supply from the country that accounted for 70% of global mine production last year has been a booster for the bombed-out cobalt price...As the supply hiatus lengthens, the impact on the cobalt processing chain grows. Even if Congo's exports resumed tomorrow, the material would only arrive in China towards the end of the first quarter of next year. A drawdown of Chinese cobalt hydroxide stocks was always anticipated, given Congo's allocated export quotas for the next two years are around half the country's previous shipping levels. But the seven-week delay since the quota announcement will serve only to accentuate the inventory depletion.

On December 3, Fastmarkets reported:

US critical minerals security drive brings new era of stockpiling, project investment.

  • The US government has ramped up its efforts to stockpile critical materials for its national stockpile, but has faced challenges in doing so.
  • Many requests by the DLA have been extended or cancelled, demonstrating the challenges of obtaining critical materials, many of which with defense applications.
  • Alongside its efforts to stockpile critical and defense metals, the US government is utilising other tools of industrial support by supporting international projects to boost domestic supply.

On December 4, the US Department of State announced:

Strategic Partnership Agreement between the government of the United States of America and the Government of the Democratic Republic of the Congo...promote secure, reliable, and mutually beneficial critical mineral flows for commercial and defense purposes between the United States of America and the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

On December 12, Fastmarkets reported:

EU’s critical minerals strategy: €3 billion boost amid industry risks...Key takeaways:

  • EU launches €3 billion plan to boost critical minerals sector and address supply chain risks.
  • Stockpiling and public-private partnerships are central to the EU’s strategy.
  • Industry experts highlight the need for investment and supply chain diversification.

On December 16, Fastmarkets reported: "CME cobalt metal futures’ annual trading volume breaks new record ahead of 5-year anniversary."

On December 19, Argus Media reported:

Viewpoint: Indonesia’s MHP surge to hit nickel prices. Indonesia is likely to expand its mixed-hydroxide-precipitate (MHP) plant capacity further in 2026, supported by record-high cobalt prices and strong production economics, a move that could deepen nickel oversupply and weigh on prices. Indonesia's MHP output is projected to reach 482,000t in nickel metal equivalent this year — almost a 50pc rise from 2024, according to Argus estimates...Refineries have been seeking cobalt alternatives because of constrained supply following export restrictions imposed by the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) since February. Indonesia's cobalt feedstock capacity is projected to hit around 65,000 t/yr in 2026, while global cobalt supply is expected to hit 210,000t over the same period, according to Argus data.

Cobalt company news

CMOC Group Limited (CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

On December 15, MarketScreener reported: "CMOC Group to buy 100% stake in EQX's Latam, Luna Gold Corp for $1.015 bln."

Glencore (GLCNF)

On December 3, Glencore announced:

Glencore Capital Markets Day 2025.

  • Significant progress on de-risking exceptional portfolio of copper assets with a pathway to produce c. 1 million tonnes by 2028 and target to produce c. 1.6 million by 2035.
  • Expected 4% overall annual compound growth rate in copper equivalent production from 2026 level to 2029, with copper production itself expected to grow at 9.4% over this period.
  • Restart of Alumbrera copper mine.
  • Streamlined operating structure with focus on accountability and ownership to deliver safe and reliable operating performance.

On December 16, Glencore announced: "Glencore strengthens presence in Peru with acquisition of the Quechua Project adjacent to its Antapaccay operation."

Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt - DRC-subsidiary Congo Dongfang International Mining

On December 16, MarketScreener reported: "Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt Signs MoU to Supply Ternary Precursors."

Jinchuan Group International Resources

On November 27, Jinchuan Group International Resources announced:

OPERATIONAL UPDATE FOR THE NINE MONTHS ENDED 30 SEPTEMBER 2025During the Period, due to the temporary suspension of the SX-EW System, the Group produced 71 tonnes of cobalt content included in cobalt hydroxide, representing a decrease of approximately 90.3% year-on-year as compared to that of 735 tonnes in the corresponding period in 2024...During the Period, the Group did not sell any cobalt as compared with 1,318 tonnes in the corresponding period in 2024 due to the low production and the export ban of cobalt since February 2025...Trading in the shares of the Company on the Stock Exchange has been suspended with effect from 9:00 a.m. on Friday, 28 March 2025 and will remain suspended until further notice.

Chemaf (subsidiary of Shalina Resources)

No news for the month.

GEM Co., Ltd.

No news for the month.

Eurasian Resources Group ("ERG") - private

ERG owns the Metalkol facility in the DRC, where ERG processes cobalt and copper tailings with a capacity of up to 24,000 tonnes of cobalt pa.

On December 19, The Astana Times reported:

ERG increases investment in Kazakhstan. Eurasian Resources Group (ERG), a Luxembourg-headquartered global metals and mining company, increased its investments in Kazakhstan to nearly $1 billion, according to the company.

On December 20, Miningdotcom reported: "ERG signs long-term gallium supply deal with Mitsubishi."

Umicore SA (UMICY)

No news for the month.

Sumitomo Metal Mining Co. (STMNF) ("SMM")

On December 19, Sumitomo Metal Mining announced: "Taganito HPAL Nickel Corporation receives an outstanding community projects award from the Philippine Economic Zone Authority."

MMC Norilsk Nickel ("Nornickel")

On December 15, Nornickel announced: "Metals Market Review." No mention of cobalt.

Sherritt International (SHERF)

On December 8, Sherritt International announced:

Leadership Transition... Leon Binedell has stepped down as Sherritt’s President and Chief Executive Officer and as a director of the Corporation, and that Dr. Peter Hancock has been appointed interim Chief Executive Officer, effective immediately... Dr. Hancock is a seasoned mining industry executive with more than 35 years of experience with Glencore plc, including overseeing nickel mining operations and developing and commercializing process technologies. He has served as an independent director of Sherritt since November 2021.

Nickel 28 (CONXF)

Nickel 28 holds an 8.56% JV interest in Ramu, which is operated by the Metallurgical Corporation of China (“MCC”).

On December 19, Nickel 28 announced: "Nickel 28 Files Fiscal Q3 2026 Financial Statements."

Electra Battery Materials (ELBM) ("Electra")

On November 25, Electra announced: "Electra signs Agreement with North American pCAM company."

Note: The company is Positive Materials Inc. (“Positive”), a Canadian-based company onshoring proven commercial precursor cathode active material (“pCAM”) manufacturing technology (“Project Positive+”) in Canada.

Other smaller cobalt producers

  • Vale SA (VALE)
  • Zijin Mining Group
  • China Nonferrous Metal Mining Company (CNMC)
  • Jiangsu Cobalt
  • Nanjing Hanrui Cobalt
  • Pengxin International Mining Co
  • Zijin Mining Group
  • Wanbau
  • GTL
  • Jiana Energy
  • Sicomes
  • Shenzhen GEM High-Tech Co

Possible mid-term producers (2027 onwards)

Sunrise Energy Metals Limited (SREMF) ("Sunrise")

Sunrise Energy Metals has 133kt of contained cobalt at their Sunrise Project. They also own the Syerston Scandium Project.

On December 16, Sunrise announced:

Follow-on Placement for A$32.5 million secured for the Syerston Scandium Project... The funds raised allow the Company to target on-site construction by mid-2026, with expansion options already under review.

Ardea Resources (ARRRF)

In total, Ardea has 6.1Mt of contained nickel and 386,000t of contained cobalt at their KNP Project near Kalgoorlie in Western Australia. The Goongarrie Hub, within the KNP, hosts 584Mt at 0.69% nickel and 0.043% cobalt for 4Mt of contained nickel and 250kt of contained cobalt. Ardea is also exploring for gold and nickel sulphide on their >5,100 km2 of 100% controlled tenements in the Eastern Goldfields region of Western Australia.

On December 8, Ardea Resources announced:

Kalgoorlie Nickel Project – Goongarrie Hub DFS Update...DFS progressing well and remains on schedule for completion in H1 CY2026...Project now based on a simplified, higher-throughput 3.5Mtpa HPAL only flowsheet. Removal of the AL circuit and autoclave optimisation for the HPAL expected to deliver higher recoveries, reduced acid consumption and thus a lower carbon footprint.

Cobalt Blue Holdings (CBBHF)—Plan to change name to Core Blue Minerals Limited

Cobalt Blue has 87kt of contained cobalt at their 100% owned Broken Hill Cobalt Project (BHCP) in NSW, Australia. LG International is an equity strategic partner. COB also has an earn-in agreement (earn in to 51% up to 75%) for the Halls Creek Project in WA (copper-lead-zinc-silver-gold). They are also developing the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery.

On December 8, Cobalt Blue announced: "Capital Raise: Successful $5.3 million institutional placement."

Australian Mines (AMSLF)

On December 3, Australian Mines announced: "Update to CEO remuneration package."

Havilah Resources (HAVRF)

Havilah 100% owns the Mutooroo copper-cobalt project about 60km west of Broken Hill in South Australia. They also have the nearby Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt Project, as well as a potentially large iron ore project at Grants. Havilah’s 100% owned Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt deposit contains JORC Mineral Resources of 1.1 million tonnes of copper, 3.1 million ounces of gold, and 23,200 tonnes of cobalt.

No news for the month.

Alliance Nickel Limited (GMRSF)

Alliance Nickel owns the NiWest Nickel-Cobalt Project located adjacent to Glencore’s Murrin Murrin Nickel operations in the North Eastern Goldfields of Western Australia. The NiWest Project has an estimated 830,000 tonnes of nickel metal and 52,000 tonnes of cobalt.

No significant news for the month.

Global Energy Metals Corp. (GBLEF) ("GEMC")

On November 28, GEMC announced:

Global Energy Metals’ partner Metal Bank Ltd. commences fully funded drilling program at the Millennium Copper Cobalt Graphite Gold Project, Australia.

Giga Metals Corp.'s (GIGGF) Turnagain Nickel-Cobalt Project is now held via the JV company Hard Creek Nickel Corporation (HNCKF) (85% Giga Metals: 15% Mitsubishi Corp. (MSBHF))

On November 25, Giga Metals announced:

Positive Geophysical results...at Turnagain... “This is the first step of a methodical program exploring for copper, platinum and palladium in the Attic Zone of the Turnagain ultramafic complex,” said Mr. Lendrum. “The MT worked well in this environment to define geometry and reveal previously unknown features... “We now have a high degree of confidence that the Turnagain intrusion geometry is in-place and up-facing, that the basal contact is untested, and the marginal contacts are untested to any significant depth.”

On December 1, Giga Metals announced: "Giga Metals engages ICP Securities Inc. for automated market making services."

TMC the metals company (TMC)

No news for the month.

Chilean Cobalt Corp. (COBA)

Chilean Cobalt Corp. (“C3”) is a critical minerals exploration and development company focused on the La Cobaltera Project located in Chile’s historic San Juan cobalt district.

On December 3, Chilean Cobalt announced:

Completion of strategic equity investment by Glencore and Madesal...raising gross proceeds of USD $3,000,000. Under the Offering, the Company issued 6,000,000 common shares ("Common Shares") at a price of $0.50 per share (the "Offering Price")...The net proceeds from the Offering will be used for exploration fieldwork at La Cobaltera and El Cofre, to pursue additional district consolidation opportunities, advance ESG diligence and continuous improvement, and for general corporate and working capital purposes.

Conclusion

December saw cobalt spot prices higher over the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Congo's attempt to control the cobalt market risks becoming a supply shock.
  • US critical minerals security drive brings new era of stockpiling, project investment.
  • Strategic Partnership Agreement between the US government and the Government of the DRC... promote secure, reliable, and mutually beneficial critical mineral flows.
  • The EU launches a €3 billion plan to boost the critical minerals sector and address supply chain risks. Stockpiling and public-private partnerships are central to the EU’s strategy.
  • CME cobalt metal futures’ annual trading volume breaks new record ahead of 5-year anniversary.
  • Indonesia's cobalt feedstock capacity is projected to hit ~65,000 t/yr in 2026; global cobalt supply is expected to hit 210,000t/yr.
  • CMOC Group to buy 100% stake in Equinox Gold's Latam, Luna Gold Corp for $1.015 bln.
  • Glencore—Significant progress on de-risking exceptional portfolio of copper assets—Pathway to produce c. 1Mtpa by 2028 and target to produce 1.6Mtpa by 2035.
  • Jinchuan Group International Resources produced 71 tonnes of cobalt in the first 9 months of 2025, down 90.3% YoY. Zero was sold.
  • ERG increases investment in Kazakhstan.
  • Sherritt International leadership transition—President & CEO Leon Binedell has stepped down; Dr. Peter Hancock has been appointed interim CEO.
  • Electra signs an agreement with North American pCAM company, Positive Materials Inc.
  • Ardea Resources—Kalgoorlie Nickel Project—Goongarrie Hub DFS is progressing well and remains on schedule for completion in H1 CY2026.
  • Giga Metals announces positive geophysical results at Turnagain.
  • Chilean Cobalt—Completion of strategic equity investment by Glencore and Madesal, raises US$3m.

As usual, all comments are welcome.

data.title: VFVA: This Regime Doesn't Favor Value Anymore
data.description:
The Vanguard U.S. Value Factor ETF Shares (VFVA), launched on 02/13/2018 and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc., provides exposure to U.S. equities with value characteristics across most size segments. It manages $750 million and charges an expense ratio of 0.13%.

When I first covered VFVA more than a year ago, I only focused on the fact that this ETF has a short track record and that the lack of a size segment focus should not favor it in the long run. In this article, I want to talk more about what makes such a fund likely to underperform in the short run due to macro conditions that should favor growth stocks.

First, it's fair to say that some value ETFs, which I only recently rated as buys, had the benefit of focusing on large caps and overweighting some of the largest U.S. corporations. This is not the case here. The top 10 holdings don't contribute to the portfolio to a very meaningful degree, and none of them is one of the largest 10 companies by market capitalization. A couple of months ago, I would have considered this an advantage due to the level of diversification, but the risk profile of the market has changed, as we'll see shortly.

The sector weights also point to a relative disadvantage now. More conservative and often considered undervalued sectors like Health Care and Financials account for a large part of the portfolio. These are likely to underperform Tech stocks, which only account for 11%.

Usually, I am able to safely assume that a value ETF can do better when uncertainty is rising and the market starts pricing some risks that it has been ignoring. This was the case back in April; it was no secret that the Trump administration was going to announce higher tariffs on imports and potentially start a trade war with other major economies. And yet, the market reacted as if it didn't take that risk seriously. Many value funds experienced more modest drawdowns than the broader equity market and growth portfolios. VFVA did not, which points to some structural difference with other value strategies.

In any case, this underperformance should persist going forward, and this is where the macro lens comes in.

First of all, even though Real GDP growth improved further in the third quarter after the contraction realized in the first one, economists still expect Real GDP to grow at a slower pace than in the recent past and long-term average levels. This is consistent with the view that the recovery in the second and third quarters is due to the deceleration of imports that followed inventory front-loading prior to the higher tariffs. Therefore, the implication is that the trend that is forming cannot be trusted in this context. The average Real GDP growth in the first half of the year was about 1.4%, and this is more reflective of the expectations that the economy is slowing after you account for the import distortion.

A more reliable trend can be observed in the index of new orders for manufacturing goods based on the related ISM survey. With only a brief recovery above 50 a few months back and the index persistently below it, the slowing aggregate demand supports the forecasts of below-average growth levels in the near term.

The direct business impact here implies a relative inferiority of value stocks. The underlying businesses are more tied to cyclical demand drivers than secular ones (e.g., Financials, Consumer Cyclical, and Energy). Sentiment doesn't shift simply because of changes in the last quarter's Real GDP figures, but the bottom-line differential should be enough to affect sentiment and punish value stocks more than growth ones in such a regime, all else being equal.

The labor market's softening further supports this regime as very likely. Nonfarm payroll growth fell from about 1.2% at the start of the year to 0.6% lately on a YoY basis. The unemployment rate has also been rising rapidly and now sits at 4.6%. Moreover, the number of unemployment insurance claims has stayed elevated and was recently reported at 224K, indicating more unemployment.

This also implies that the Federal Reserve is going to keep cutting at the new pace it has stuck to since September. The labor market is now the bigger risk, and monetary policy should prioritize it over inflation. Besides, CPI and Core CPI YoY growth seem to have stabilized below 3%. The idea was that with the new tariff rates, businesses were going to pass on the higher input cost to consumers as soon as the pre-tariff inventory ran out. The trend after April reflects this assumption, but the level is now closer to the Fed's target of 2% and helps to allow the central bank to be less concerned about inflation getting out of control again if it cuts further.

This is not a headwind for value stocks, of course, but this outlook favors growth stocks more, as they are more sensitive to interest rate changes.

Before the higher tariffs were enacted and the trade wars, the case for value strategies was stronger primarily because of sentiment and sticky inflation that maintained the high uncertainty. Now that the market is desensitized to some extent and the labor market is obviously the bigger risk for the Fed, value stocks have lost their edge. It's highly unlikely that these conditions will allow value portfolios to outperform the broader market and growth stocks in the near term. For this reason, I am maintaining my hold rating on VFVA.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: Gift BOXX Under The Tree Brings Holiday Cheers And No Tax Fears
data.description:
The Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX) is best understood as a short-term interest rate instrument wrapped in a tax-efficient ETF wrapper. It seeks to “equal or exceed the price and yield performance” of the 1–3 month U.S. Treasury bill sector, before fees and expenses. Instead of owning T-bills, it gets there by packaging box spreads, a multi-leg options strategy, so the fund’s return shows up primarily as NAV accretion and price appreciation rather than a steady stream of taxable interest distributions.

BOXX invests in box spreads with a weighted average maturity of less than 90 days. The fund expects to trade some or all positions prior to expiration and roll into new spreads. The prospectus also states the fund uses European-style options, which matters because European-style options can be exercised only at expiration, eliminating early assignment risk. So we can say that BOXX is like a ladder of very short-term “synthetic bills,” refreshed continuously.

Understanding Box Spreads

A box spread uses put-call parity to engineer a guaranteed fixed payoff at maturity. Conceptually, you create two vertical spreads with the same strikes and expiry: a bull call spread and a bear put spread. Note also that these must be European options so that there is no risk of early assignment, and in nearly all cases we are using SPX index options because these are the most liquid European options. Since some legs of the box spread are always going to be ITM, this is a very important stipulation

If you choose strikes K_1 and K_2, where K_1 < K_2, and hold the combined position to expiry, the payoff is the same regardless of where the underlying ends up: it is simply K_2 – K_1. The New York Fed walks through this intuition in this article, noting that the combined payoff at maturity from the four-leg structure is constant and equal to the strike difference.

Once the payoff is fixed, the “interest rate” shows up in the price you pay today. You pay less than K_2 – K_1 today and receive K_2 – K_1 at maturity, and the difference is your implied lending return. Pay more today (possible in some market microstructure contexts), and you’ve effectively borrowed. The return of a box spread is basically the implied interest rate of engaging in this type of financial markets transaction.

Based on the no-arbitrage principle, that implied rate should sit very close to the risk-free rate for the same maturity because arbitrage should collapse persistent gaps. In practice, it won’t match T-bills perfectly. The “box spread rate” is an options market funding rate influenced by the supply and demand for financing through options, bid-ask spreads and market liquidity, and the mechanics of margins and clearing. In contrast to SPX options, T-bills are the most liquid instruments in the world and are viewed under various frameworks as high-quality liquid assets. That’s why box spread rates often trade rich to T-bills, as seen in this chart below. An illiquidity spread is applied to the box spread.

BOXX’s job is to harvest that box spread rate consistently at the front end of the curve and do it efficiently enough that, after fees and trading frictions, investors receive economic returns like that of a T-bill. One advantage of BOXX is that because the gains accrue to the NAV and are not distributed regularly, it actually offers better tax treatment compared to T-bills. Consider that holding a ladder of less than 3-month bills will incur a tax burden on the interest paid in the same year that it is paid. ETFs that hold floating rate notes or roll over short-term paper and pay out a steady dividend have the same problem. BOXX does not have this problem.

Tailwinds and Headwinds for BOXX

Because BOXX is economically a short-term rates instrument, macro factors matter more through the path of front-end rates, money-market plumbing, and the slope of the very short curve.

The Fed cut on December 10, 2025, and set the target range for the federal funds rate to 3.50%–3.75%. In the same neighborhood, FRED shows SOFR at 3.68% for December 22, 2025, and the three-month constant-maturity Treasury yield has been around the mid-3s recently. In that environment, BOXX should deliver “cash-like” returns in the mid-3s to high-3s before fees, with the exact realized number dependent on box spread rate execution, roll timing, and trading costs.

If the Fed continues easing, BOXX’s forward return compresses quickly. That’s a headwind in absolute terms (lower returns), and it is a headwind in relative terms because the expense ratio becomes a larger share of the gross yield. When cash yields are 0–1%, a ~0.19% net expense ratio becomes a meaningful tax on the strategy.

If the Fed pauses at restrictive-or-neutral levels, BOXX is in its sweet spot. “Higher for longer” keeps the gross carry high enough that net returns remain competitive after fees, while the low duration profile limits mark-to-market swings.

If inflation re-accelerates and the Fed hikes again, BOXX benefits. But the subtle risk is that hiking cycles can coincide with periodic money-market stress. In those episodes, the spread between box spread rates and T-bills can widen, and liquidity premia can show up in option financing. That doesn’t necessarily mean BOXX loses money—as long as the box spread is held several weeks to maturity and the OCC does not fail, it is guaranteed to pay off as expected—but it does mean tracking can become noisier at exactly the moments investors are most sensitive to “cash behaving oddly.”

During severe dislocations, bid-ask spreads widen and rolling costs can increase. The payoff of a held-to-expiry box is fixed, but the fund’s actual implementation includes rolling and active trading, so short-term frictions matter more than they would for someone who personally put on a box spread and held it to maturity. To be clear, I don’t see this as a real concern, but it is something that is good to be aware of.

Ultimately the counterparty for options is the OCC, which is recognized as a systemically important financial market utility (SIFMU). This is highly, highly unlikely to have serious issues.

Tax Mechanics

As I mentioned, BOXX’s differentiator is its tax packaging. Instead of distributing interest, it allows NAV to rise, so holders realize taxable gain mostly when they sell, potentially as capital gains rather than ordinary income. For investors with taxable cash allocations—especially those who can hold longer than a year—tax deferral plus long-term capital gains treatment can be meaningfully better than current taxation of money market distributions.

BOXX is structured as a regulated investment company. Under the RIC rules, the shareholder is usually the primary taxpayer, not the fund—provided the fund meets the qualification and distribution requirements. ETFs also have a structural advantage that can reduce realized gains via the in-kind creation and redemption mechanism. When an ETF meets redemptions by handing out securities in kind, it can avoid realizing gains inside the fund, avoiding having to distribute gains to shareholders in the current year.

Where BOXX becomes unusual is that its return comes from box spreads. The tax outcome depends on which options it uses in the in-kind creation and redemption process. Index options are treated as Section 1256 contracts, which have a 60/40 split between long- and short-term capital gains. This also forces annual recognition of gains and losses for those contracts, which increases the odds of taxable distributions.

So what does BOXX do?

The answer is it selects certain contracts as part of its strategy in the in-kind creation and redemption process so as to manage the realizations. What ends up happening is that the fund does not have taxable gains even though it has consistent economic gains because it realizes losses while using in-kind transactions to not have to realize gains.

Overall, this is a really impressive use of the tax code to engineer a fund that can consistently grow at the risk-free rate without incurring regular taxes.

The larger issue is the durability and longevity of this situation. The IRS could close this “loophole” and potentially cause the fund to have to pass off distributions. Should the tax regime change, the basic economics of BOXX could be slightly altered.

BOXX itself flags this kind of risk in its prospectus, and ETF.com describes the strategy as tax arbitrage that could face a regulatory response.

At this point, I would also like to note that because BOXX ultimately uses Section 1256 contracts (SPX options) to comprise the box spreads, any future distributions—if mandated by a change in the tax code—would pass the 60/40 treatment through to the investor. This would still make it superior to the likes of SGOV, USFR, and TFLO (which also pay short-term risk-free rates), which would be taxed as ordinary income, which is effectively the short-term capital gains tax rate.

Overall, the slightly higher rate on box spreads vs. treasuries has allowed it to do better than competitors over the last year. This is on a net of fees but before tax basis, so the after-tax basis is even better.

In summary, BOXX’s “NAV compounding” is enabled by its unique structure and the way options returns can be packaged via in-kind transactions, but the edge ultimately rests on tax law remaining favorable. However, even in an unfavorable tax regime, the use of index options will still create a slightly better tax treatment.

Risks

As mentioned, the investor is exposed to the OCC as the ultimate counterparty. This is perhaps worse than the U.S. government, which is what you’d get with T-bills. However, the OCC is systemically important, and that is a major margin of safety.

Second, BOXX could engage in active and frequent trading and expects high portfolio turnover. This has some execution risk. It is also slightly exposed to liquidity risk. If spreads widen from systemic stress, you can lose many basis points here and there, which could lead to underperformance against T-bills.

Third, the tax feature could change. If the IRS narrows the advantage, BOXX becomes a higher-fee, short-term, risk-free rate product and loses its edge versus SGOV.

Finally, there is the macro risk of lower rates at the front of the curve. With a higher fee, the investor nets a smaller portion of total returns.

Conclusion

BOXX is a clever wrapper around a well-understood options trade, and in today’s mid-3% short-term rates environment, it should behave like a useful cash surrogate. The fund’s defining edge is its highly favorable tax treatment. In that sense, it stands out as the best near-zero volatility, positive returns asset, beating out competitors like SGOV, USFR, and TFLO.

Obviously, BOXX will not outperform things with greater risk premia and growth potential. If rates fall, it should heavily underperform risk assets. But because I think BOXX is truly the best of its own category within the investment universe, I give it a Buy rating.

data.title: Preparing For An Anemic 2026 Market
data.description:
Introduction

In January of each year, I perform an annual assessment of my overall portfolio and rebalance. Anyone who has studied risk management understands that to manage risk, one should do occasional rebalancing. First, I have to answer up to 19 questions from The Smart Money Guide to Long-Term Investing: How to Build Real Wealth for Retirement and Other Future Goals (2003), by Nellie Huang and Peter Finch. I used their questions, adapted the results to align with more modern allocation theories, and developed an allocation tool that I use for myself and anyone who asks for my help.

Here we go.

The Questions

These questions must be answered before anyone can understand their needs and how to allocate their money. Excuse my brief rant. Advisors who are not asking these kinds of questions give you a short survey, and show a card with six sample portfolios need to be fired. The job of an advisor is to understand the answers to the following questions.

Initial Considerations

  • How old are you? This is a no-brainer. Once one reaches age 59 ½, the need for a fixed income increases with each passing year. For each year beyond 59 ½, add one percentage point to their fixed-income allocation.
  • When will you need the money? Another obvious question. If one’s retirement age is less than 30 years away, then they should take less risk with each year that passes. That means that as one approaches retirement, they need to add 1% to their fixed-income allocation. If retirement is more than 30 years away, make no changes.
  • Portfolio requirements will change your overall allocation and reduce your fixed-income and equity allocations.
    • Will you consider hedge funds? I suggest no more than 15% is invested in hedge funds.
    • Will you consider REITs? I suggest no more than 6% toward real estate.
    • Will you consider gold and other commodities? I suggest no more than 6% in gold.
    • Will you consider international investments? For years, global equities have lagged, but 2025 proved that one should consider investing beyond the United States.

How Much Fixed Income

How one answers these questions will change one’s fixed-income needs. I hope I can explain it well. Please note that anyone with a pension will need to make significant adjustments to these calculations, since a pension functions as a fixed income.

  • What is the size of your portfolio? If one’s portfolio is greater than $640,000, then no adjustment is necessary for their fixed-income requirements. If it is less than $128,000, they need to add 3% to their fixed-income allocation. For any amount in between those amounts, they need to add 1%.
  • How much are you contributing toward investing per year? If you contribute more than $24,000 per year, no fixed-income adjustment is necessary. If you contribute only up to $7,500 per year, then add 2% to your fixed income. For any other amount, add 1%.
  • What percent of your portfolio is in tax-deferred accounts? If the answer to this question is less than 25%, then add 1% to your fixed-income allocation. If it exceeds 75%, add 3% to your fixed income. For any other percentage, add 2%.

Spending Needs

It is essential to understand how much of one's money will be needed over the next 10 years. Here are the questions that need to be answered.

  • How many people, such as a child, spouse, or aging parent, are financially dependent on you? If you have dependents, add 1% to your fixed income for each dependent, up to a maximum of 3.
  • What percentage of your investments will you spend in the next three years? If this answer is zero, then reduce your fixed income by 1%. If it is less than 9%, add 1%. If it is between 9% and 18%, add 5%. If it is more than 18%, add 8%.
  • What percentage of your investments will you spend in the next ten years? If this answer is zero, then reduce your fixed income by 1%. If it is less than 30%, make no changes. If it is between 30% and 60%, add 4%. If it is more than 60%, then add 6%.
  • How much equity do you have in your home? If you are a renter, add 2% to your fixed-income allocation. Once you buy a house and take on debt, the equation changes a little. If you own less than 1/3 of the home’s equity, add 4%. If you own more than 1/3, then add 1%. If you own your home free and clear, then make no changes.

Current Income

This is pretty basic. If you are in a low-income tax bracket, add 3% to your fixed income. If you are in a middle-income tax bracket, add 2%. High-income earners do not have to make any changes.

Volatility Tolerance

This is important. If you do not know your risk tolerance, I suggest taking the Grable & Lytton Risk Tolerance Scale. It is an excellent assessment and has been peer-reviewed for its validity. After you take the quiz, you will find out whether you are:

  • Very Conservative—add 10% to fixed income
  • Conservative—add 6% to fixed income
  • Moderate—add 2% to fixed income
  • Aggressive—subtract 2.5% from fixed income
  • Very Aggressive-subtract 7% from fixed income

Economic Outlook

This will be a somewhat personal question, but it should be rooted in evidence. How one views the economy and markets over the next 12 months will affect their fixed income allocation. These are the adjustments one should make.

  • Turning Down—add 8% to fixed income
  • Anemic Growth—add 4% to fixed income
  • Strong Low Inflation—add 3% to fixed income
  • Strong High Inflation—add 2% to fixed income
  • Don’t Know—make no changes. In normal circumstances, this is my default choice, but I believe 2026 will be anemic.

A lot of math and algorithms take over from here when I run this tool for myself, and they are based on the work of Huang and Finch. The same questions above also determine how much I will allocate toward large caps, mid-caps, small caps, and foreign equities. As for the alternative investments—gold, real estate, and hedge funds—they are proportional to my age relative to my retirement timeframe, fixed-income allocation, and equity allocation.

I plan to retire at the end of the year, and I consider myself an aggressive investor. We also own our home free and clear. Reserving any additional information, this is the allocation that is generated for me. It includes 46% in cash, cash equivalents, and fixed income.

  • Cash—4%
  • Cash Equivalents—15%
  • Investment Grade Bonds—9%
  • High Yield Bonds—9%
  • International Bonds—9%
  • Large-Cap Stocks—11%
  • Mid-Cap Stocks—10%

  • Developed Markets—8%
  • Emerging Markets—4%
  • Real Estate—2%
  • Gold or Precious Metals—2%
  • Hedge Funds—8%

I am satisfied with the outcome of this allocation when I use the relevant indices for January 1995 to October 2025. If you will allow me to brag, these numbers make this an excellent allocation.

data.title: The Pace Of Acquisitions By TowneBank Creates Both Opportunity And Risk
data.description:
This year has certainly been one for acquisitions in the banking sector. A recent update from earlier this month placed the total number of banking deals in 2025 at over 150, with the combined value of these transactions in October, $21.4 billion, being the highest monthly total since early 2019. Just two days ago, I wrote about the December 18 announcement by Burke & Herbert Financial Services (BHRB) to acquire LINKBANCORP (LNKB) in an all-stock transaction worth about $354 million.

If it is not the most active bank in the merger space this year, TowneBank (TOWN) certainly ranks near the top. The Virginia-based regional bank closed on a $120 million deal to acquire Village Bank and Trust on April 1. Just two days later, it announced its intent to buy Old Point Financial for just over $200 million. That transaction closed at the start of September. As if that was not enough turbulence for the bank's financial reports, on August 19, TOWN stated that it would buy North Carolina-based Dogwood State Bank (DSBX). That deal is expected to be larger than the other two combined, with a value of roughly $475 based on the value of TowneBank's stock on the date of closing.

Without complete consideration for the financial ramifications of these actions, there are pros and cons to be considered for the bank's actions. On one hand, TOWN will likely soon have one of the more attractive geographic footprints in Virginia and the Carolinas. The bank is clearly increasing its presence in high-growth markets, which could make it an attractive acquisition candidate itself in the near future. On the other hand, the expenses involved in these deals, including the issuance of stock, along with the widening gap between TOWN's share price and its intrinsic value, are starting to create some red flags in my mind.

Company Overview

TowneBank offers a full range of banking services through over 50 branches located in Central and Eastern Virginia as well as Northeastern and Central North Carolina. More than just a typical regional bank, TOWN also controls a number of complementary financial subsidiaries, including Towne Wealth Management, Towne Insurance Agency, and TowneBank Mortgage. Towne Insurance Agency is the largest bank-owned insurance company in the US. The bank also operates a property management division that serves properties in North Carolina, South Carolina, Maryland, Tennessee, and Florida.

A current map of the bank's service area is shown below from its most recent quarterly earnings report. With the addition of the Old Point Financial locations, TOWN now holds the largest market share in the Hampton Roads area. This area, which includes Virginia Beach, Norfolk, and Hampton, is the second most populous area in the state behind Northern Virginia.

As of December 23, TOWN had a market cap of $2.72 billion. The bank controlled total assets of $19.7 billion. That total was 14.5% higher than at the end of 2024, but it did include the impact of the two acquisitions that had closed by the end of Q3 2025.

Total loans for the bank had a value of $13.4 billion, an increase of 16.5% from the end of 2024. Almost half of those loans were connected to commercial real estate. At the end of last year, the bank's concentration in CRE was 49%, so the recent additions to its balance sheet have made little change to its diversification. Residential mortgages make up the second-highest allocation in TOWN's loan mix at 21%. Commercial and industrial loans are third, and the only other loan type with a weight of at least 10%.

The loan portfolio for TOWN only makes up 68% of the bank's assets. TowneBank has almost $1 billion in deposits at the FRB, as well as a portfolio of securities worth $2.8 billion. Those securities account for an additional 14.5% of TOWN's assets. Over half of this portfolio is committed to mortgage-backed securities. TowneBank expects to receive $966 million in cash flow from this portfolio in the next two years, with $382 million coming within the next 12 months.

The total deposits held by TOWN, $16.3 billion, have increased 14.5% since the end of Q4 2024. The bank benefits from 31% of its deposits being held in non-interest-bearing accounts. This places the bank well above its peers, as shown in the graph below. It also helps reduce the overall cost of deposits for TowneBank, which was measured at 1.75% at the end of Q3. The national average for a bank of its size is 2.24%, per the FDIC.

TOWN currently yields 3.09%, having grown its annual payout for 13 straight years. Its final distribution for 2025 will actually be made on January 7, 2026, for stockholders of record on December 26. The last dividend increase for the company was announced on May 14, when TOWN raised its quarterly payout by 8%, from $0.25 to $0.27.

Analysis of TowneBank

On October 23, TowneBank released its Q3 2025 financial results, which included earnings of $39.0 million, or $0.51 per diluted share. Core earnings, which leave out many of the effects of the bank's recent transactions, were $0.83 per share, $0.07 ahead of expectations. Revenue of $215.7 million was a 23.6% improvement from Q3 2024 but missed previous projections by 2.3%.

When the costs of acquisitions are removed from the equation, earnings growth for TOWN in 2025 has been more significant. The bank has reported $2.32 per share in non-GAAP profit in the first nine months of this year after netting just $1.63 during the same time period in 2024. Analysts expect TowneBank to record a core profit of $2.95 per share this year and $3.60 next year, a 22% gain year-over-year. Given that the stock currently trades at a PE of 11.83 for FY 2025 earnings, the valuation looks reasonable at first glance. This 22% growth in earnings is actually in line with the compound average growth rate for earnings for TOWN dating back to FY 2000, which the bank reports to be 22.7%.

As mentioned in the company overview, TowneBank has more than just the usual banking operations. The company also gets meaningful financial contributions from its wealth management, insurance, home mortgage, and property management divisions. A full account of TOWN's non-interest income is shown below. On a comparative basis with Q3 2024, the company's residential mortgage and property management reported an income decrease of 3.2% and 13.0%, respectively. However, for the first nine months, all divisions are ahead of their pace from 2024, with property management actually showing a double-digit improvement.

Given the recent additions to the bank, I expected to see more issues with its loan quality than what I found in its most recent quarterly report. TowneBank did increase its allowance for credit losses in Q3 by about $15 million when compared to Q2. About $13.5 million of that was related to the Old Point Financial acquisition. ACL to total loans is now 1.11% after being 1.09% in Q2.

However, the condition of the bank's loan assets appears to be relatively consistent this year. Non-performing assets have been in the range of 0.04% to 0.05% for the last three years. Net charge-offs have declined significantly since 2023. Criticized loans have increased in their total value this year, but they have declined slightly this year in proportion to the size of the total loan portfolio.

Dogwood State Bank Details

Dogwood State Bank is headquartered in Raleigh, NC. The company operates 16 branches in the Carolinas and one in the northeast corner of Tennessee. A look at the pro forma footprint for TowneBank post-merger is shown below. The financial highlights for DSBX provided by TOWN as part of its Q3 earnings presentation show the new addition to be a high-performing institution with return on assets and net interest margin ahead of the national average for a bank of its size. The adjusted efficiency ratio for the most recent quarter shown was just below the national average.

A look at the combined loan portfolios and deposit mixes for the banks is shown below. Once the deal is completed, TOWN will control $18.6 billion in deposits and $15.5 billion in loans. The deal will increase TOWN's yield on loans by 15 basis points while also increasing its cost of deposits by 6 basis points.

The deal will boost other aspects of the bank's profitability metrics by the end of next year. TOWN projects that the combination of operations will raise its return on assets by 10 basis points, its return on tangible common equity by 130 basis points, and its net interest margin by 0.17%. If these improvements come to fruition, it would place TowneBank well above the median of its peers.

DSBX shareholders will receive 0.7 shares of TOWN when the deal closes. TowneBank is paying 2.12x tangible book value for Dogwood State Bank. The company states that the pricing for similar deals in the Carolinas has been 2.29x. However, the industry median for takeovers is around 1.5x. A recent report from S&P Global says this takeover has the third-highest premium out of the 20 largest bank deals from 2025. Another metric used to measure bank deals is the size of the deposits for the bank being acquired. The norm is that the buyout price is around 16.6% for those deposits. TOWN is paying 24%.

The justification for this is the geography. The Carolinas are a strong market for regional banks. A recent federal report showed that South Carolina has the top-performing economy in the US. I wrote an analysis in October about Greenville-based Southern First Bancshares (SFST), calling its stock a Buy. Since that article was published, SFST has gained almost 26%.

I am still grappling with the valuation for DSBX, however, and the impact this will have on TowneBank. TOWN states the deal will dilute its tangible book value by roughly 6% and take four years to earn back. At the end of Q3 2025, TowneBank had a tangible book value of $21.59, $0.06 lower than at the end of Q3 2024. When TOWN's tangible book value takes that hit post-merger, it will trade at 1.71x TBV, above that 1.5x national average for acquisitions.

Additional Risks to Consider

The merger activity for TOWN over the last 12 months is certainly greater than what most banks will go through in several years. There are always risks that the transactions will not yield their anticipated benefits. Costs could be higher than expected, and the integration of bank operations may not go smoothly. There are no guarantees that the Dogwood State Bank acquisition will be approved by regulators, but I do think the risk of termination is small. Part of the reason that mergers have increased in frequency this year is that the current administration in Washington, DC, is much more amenable toward the financial sector.

As with all regional banks, TowneBank would be adversely affected by a downturn in the overall US economy, rising inflation, and falling consumer confidence. Higher interest rates and lower retail sales could provide headwinds for the bank's sizeable commercial real estate loan portfolio. Higher mortgage rates could reduce opportunities for the bank to produce new residential loans. Loan defaults of either commercial or residential real estate could place the bank in the position of having to manage properties and/or sell them at a loss.

Seeking Alpha's Quant analysis considers TOWN to be a Sell. The stock has actually received a negative rating for most of the second half of this year. TowneBank earns low scores in Profitability, Momentum, and Revisions. Three analysts have reduced their earnings expectations for the company in the last three months. Two have cut their revenue estimates as well.

For those who believe in the seasonality of stock prices, January is a notoriously bad month for TowneBank. Its stock has only had two winning starts to the year since 2015. The 10-year average loss for January for TOWN is 2.1%. There have been no months that have averaged gains for the company until April since 2015.

The Bottom Line

There are a lot of moving parts at TowneBank, and the relatively consistent flow of merger-related charges this year has made comparisons for the bank more challenging. There is no doubt that the company has a strong history of positive returns. Its footprint and strong presence in key metropolitan areas in the Southeast also make TOWN more attractive. I do not think it should be overlooked that the Dogwood acquisition gives the bank access to new markets in Tennessee as well as South Carolina.

Nonetheless, there is too much noise around the company right now to have enough optimism for a Buy rating at this time. The dividend gives some relief to the expected turbulence that may lie ahead. I consider TowneBank to be a Hold heading into 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MarineMax: The Ride Risks Getting Bumpy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in July of this year, I made the decision to reaffirm MarineMax (HZO) as a ‘buy’ candidate. I acknowledged at the time that challenging market conditions existed. However, the stock was attractively priced, and the moves that management was making were promising. At the end of the day, I believed that these positives would outweigh the negatives. But since then, the stock has dropped 6.8%, while the S&P 500 is up 7.4%. This return disparity can be attributed to weakening revenue, profits, and cash flows. The good news is that management expects 2026 to come in stronger than 2025 did. But I would be surprised if that transpires. I think that the economic climate will be worse in the first half of next year than what would be needed to achieve robust financial performance. And based on how the stock is currently priced, I think it would be more appropriate to take a more cautious approach and downgrade it to a ‘hold.’

I’m Not Taking a Ride on This Anymore

Perhaps the best place to start with when it comes to MarineMax would be to cover results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, the financial performance for the business was really not that great. Revenue came in at $552.2 million. That represents a decline from the $563.1 million the company reported a year earlier. Management attributed this to lower new boat sales, as well as to certain portfolio rationalization efforts. These rationalization efforts involved strategic store closures that occurred since the end of 2024. Since the summer of the 2024 fiscal year, the company has closed ten of its stores. As disappointing as this was, not everything was bad. In fact, same store sales grew by 2.3%. But context is important here. Management attributed this to used boat revenue, finance and insurance products, parts and services income, and an undisclosed amount of benefit from its Superyacht services and marina operations.

These details are important. If new boat sales are weakening and used boat sales are rising, that does show some demand. But it shows that customers are overwhelmingly focused on indulging in these purchases without breaking the bank. This on its own is a type of resiliency that should be looked upon favorably. Parts and service income and the other service-oriented offerings of the company saw growth tied into either this used boat theme or to the super high-end clientele that is not as affected by current market conditions as everyone else. The finance and insurance revenue increase very well could have come from persistently high interest rates. But unfortunately, management did not comment on this during their final quarter earnings call.

With revenue falling, it should come as no surprise that profitability would decline also. The firm went from a net profit of $4 million to a net loss of $0.9 million. Adjusted net profits came in worse, dropping from $5.5 million to negative $0.9 million. We did see operating cash flow skyrocket from negative $0.8 million to $61.4 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a drop from $24.7 million to $14.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company declined from $33.5 million to $17.3 million.

It is worth noting that a lot of the bottom line pain was in spite of the fact that the gross profit margin for the company improved. In my previous article earlier this year, I highlighted how management was shifting toward higher margin services. And that seems to have paid off here, with management specifically mentioning that higher margin businesses have performed well and have worked to offset ‘significantly lower boat margins’ caused by the current challenging retail environment. It was only because of selling, general, and administrative costs rising that the picture worsened from a profit margin perspective. Targeted marketing investments aimed at boosting sales in a tough environment, as well as greater service-related revenue that has different costs than retail store operations, can be blamed for this.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows all worsened. And this is a testament, unfortunately, to the difficult environment that companies in the recreational boating space are dealing with. Interestingly, management does seem optimistic about next year. They expect, as an example, for EBITDA to be between $110 million and $125 million. For context, in 2025, it amounted to $109.8 million.

Management seems confident that continued diversification into the higher margin opportunities that exist out there will be instrumental in combating other challenges such as weak consumer spending, high tariffs, and other issues. This does not mean that the company doesn't have the opportunity to boost some unit sales. Management even characterized the results experienced at the Fort Lauderdale Boat Show as positive, indicating the demand for the boating lifestyle is ‘resilient.’

Management is not the only one that is optimistic about next year. According to one source, for the first half of 2025, new retail powerboat sales were down 9.7% compared to the same time last year. But that source also said that, as we head into 2026, inventories have come under control and there have been improvements in the health of supply chains. But broader economic uncertainty, combined with worries over a high cost of living and high interest rates, needs to be factored in. One big question will likely be whether or not to wait for rate cuts since we are now in a pattern of declining interest rates courtesy of the Federal Reserve.

The National Marine Manufacturers Association, or NMMA for short, has warned that 2026 could see big ticket sales experience more friction. Demand is currently soft but stable, according to them. But with broader economic conditions still proving to be a challenge, they are clearly cautious. Interestingly, they also mentioned that workforce and demand expectations are positive, with hiring plans in the industry on the rise. Demand expectations seem to have normalized also. But as I have detailed in other articles, I actually think that the US economy is barreling toward a recession. So I am hesitant to be all that optimistic myself. In fact, even though I think interest rates will fall faster than what most people expect, I believe that the first half of next year is unlikely to be pretty.

Assuming that management is correct about expectations for next year when it comes to EBITDA and that we use the midpoint of estimates there, adjusted operating cash flow should come in at around $75.2 million. Using these estimates and also using the historical results that the company saw last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These multiples aren't bad. But considering the climate that we are in, a weakening could make the company more expensive rather quickly. Relative to other similar firms, MarineMax is priced at levels that are mixed. In that same chart, I compared it to four similar firms. And what I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the four firms was cheaper than it. But on an EV to EBITDA basis, three of the companies are cheaper than it is.

Takeaway

As much as it pains me to do this, I believe that taking a more cautious approach when it comes to MarineMax is necessary right now. Results have been weakening, and even though management is optimistic about the future, I am more pessimistic. Eventually, industry conditions will work things out. Demand will eventually come back. But with a forward net leverage ratio of 7.98, this prospect is rather heavily indebted. This justifies, in my view, downgrading it to a ‘hold.’

data.title: Columbia Short Duration Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the third quarter of 2025.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Short Duration Municipal Bond Fund returned 1.62% for the three months ending September 30, 2025. For monthly performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com .
  • The fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg 1-5 Year Municipal Bond Index, returned 1.53% for the same period.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter, as the fund's security selection and sector allocation were the primary drivers.

Market overview

The third quarter began with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act clearing both chambers of Congress and being signed into law on July 4 by President Trump. Throughout the quarter, tariff policy headlines continued, yet market reactions to trade policy announcements decreased relative to the previous quarter. While inflation and growth uncertainty remained, the U.S. Federal Reserve shifted its focus to the labor market, which showed signs of weakness throughout the period. The Fed viewed the downside risks to employment as meriting some degree of easing, despite inflation that remained somewhat elevated. After leaving its benchmark overnight lending rate unchanged at its August meeting, the Fed lowered the federal funds target rate in mid-September by a quarter point, to the 4.00%–4.25% range. Returns were meaningfully positive across all sectors of the U.S. bond market for the quarter. Treasury yields ended the quarter slightly lower across the yield curve, as reflected in the decline of the 10-year Treasury yield from 4.24% to 4.16% over the quarter.

Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the quarter. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 3.00%, while Treasuries were up 1.51% over the same period. Lower-quality municipals lagged higher-quality municipals as the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index returned 1.63% during the quarter, bringing its year-to-date return to 1.29%. A dominant theme from the first half of 2025 was the reshaping and ultimate steepening of the municipal yield curve. During the third quarter, the 1–30-year AAA slope flattened by 11 basis points (bps). (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) Longer-maturity municipal bonds materially outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts.

On the technical front, supply continued to expand at a record-setting pace. New issuance totaled $150 billion during the quarter, 6% higher year over year. The typically strong summer-month technicals were late to materialize, although the market's seasonality has not performed according to the usual pattern this year. After a modestly negatively second quarter in terms of flows, fund flows increased throughout the third quarter. Open-ended funds experienced $8.3 billion in inflows, while exchange traded funds (ETFs) saw $10.1 billion in inflows for the quarter. The short and intermediate portions of the yield curve benefited most from the positive flows.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Security selection and sector allocation were the primary drivers of the fund's outperformance. An underweight to state general obligation (GOs) and overweights to industrial development revenue (IDR) and housing bonds were additive to returns, while an underweight to pre-pay gas and an overweight to charter schools slightly detracted. Security selection in airport, pre-pay gas, utility and leasing bonds were positive contributors, while issue selection within IDR, special tax and hospital bonds detracted from returns. Quality allocation, with overweights to both the AA and A rated segments of the market was additive, while other quality segments were roughly flat in terms of relative contribution. Trading activity for the period was focused on an opportunistic approach to re-deploy cash from principal and interest payments. Outlook

We remain optimistic on our outlook for the municipal market, while also noting the fall season typically brings a more-challenging technical environment, with the lack of reinvestment from coupon and interest payments. We also expect issuance will continue at a record pace, and we remain optimistic about locking in attractive opportunities at elevated yields. Municipal/Treasury yield ratios are mixed, yet are attractive for potential crossover buyers, especially in longer maturities. While credit spreads remain compressed, we continue to prefer higher-quality over lower-quality credits, particularly as we see some areas of economic weakness and cracks beginning to emerge in the labor market. Fundamentals remain on solid footing. However, default rates are picking up slightly (albeit from very low levels), so credit research and active management will remain imperative to success.

data.title: TriplePoint Venture Growth: Be Careful With This 15% Yield
data.description:
TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) has lowered its dividend twice since Q2 2024: it lowered its dividend from $0.40 per share in Q2 2024 to $0.23 per share in Q4 2025, registering a total decline of 43%. Despite this massive decrease in the regular dividend, the investment firm still struggles with performance issues in its core lending portfolio. The BDC had a non-accrual ratio way above 3% in the most recent quarter, and while the dividend coverage trend has most recently improved, questions remain as to whether TPVG could narrow the gap between share price and book value in 2026. Given the BDC's past record of dividend cuts, I currently rate TriplePoint Venture Growth as a hold only.

Previous rating

I rated shares of TriplePoint Venture Growth a hold in my last work on the BDC in August – Not Worth The Risk Right Now – despite persistent NII coverage issues because I hoped that the investment firm would be able to work through its very high non-accruals. Since the Federal Reserve has lowered federal fund rates in December, and more rate cuts are on the table in 2026, there is a good chance for TriplePoint Venture Growth to see falling NII next year. While shares now trade at a 30% discount to net asset value, I don't see an enticing value offer.

TPVG’s non-accrual setup is still bad

TriplePoint Venture Growth is a venture capital-focused BDC that operates in a niche segment in the broader middle market segment. Like BDCs such as Hercules Capital (HTGC) or Trinity Capital (TRIN), TriplePoint Venture Growth originates loans mostly in the technology and life science sectors.

The focus on fast-growing technology companies distinguishes these BDCs from traditional lenders, as they prioritize growth in enterprise value and future growth potential over current cash flow. As a result, companies like TPVG or HTGC are interested in taking equity positions in anticipation of a major liquidity event, such as a buyout or an IPO.

Despite its orientation towards growth companies, TriplePoint Venture Growth has seen a rather steep contraction in its net investment income in the last year, mainly due to a rise in non-performing loans. In the most recent quarter, TPVG generated only $10.3M in net investment income, showing a decline rate of 25% year-over-year. Most importantly, with interest rates set to drop further in 2026, TriplePoint Venture Growth is going to see at least some NII pressure. While TPVG has a lower-than-average variable rate percentage, however, the BDC could see less earnings downside than some of its tech-oriented rivals.

In the last two years, TriplePoint Venture Growth has seen a significant increase in its non-accruals, which led to a massive drop-off in the investment firm's net asset value as well as net investment income. The main problem for TPVG is that the BDC originated non-performing loans that are now a drag on its performance. In the most recent quarter, the investment firm posted a non-accrual ratio of 3.7% based off of fair value, which is significantly above the industry group average.

In a direct comparison to BDC rivals HTGC and TRIN, TPVG is comparing well on a number of metrics. While the BDC has by far the highest non-accrual ratio of 3.7%, TriplePoint Venture Growth had only a 66.1% variable rate percentage, which is going to make the BDC less vulnerable to rate cuts on the part of the Federal Reserve next year.

Additionally, because TriplePoint Venture Growth already instituted two dividend cuts in total since Q2 2024, the investment firm has much better dividend coverage than either HTGC or TRIN. TriplePoint Venture Growth had a Q3 2025 dividend coverage ratio of 113%, which is the second-best ratio in the industry group: Hercules Capital still has the best NII coverage, with a ratio of 122.5%, while Trinity Capital currently has the lowest coverage in the industry group of 102%.

Trinity Capital, however, has a very competitive non-accrual ratio of 1.0% – which is about on the same level as Hercules Capital's – and the BDC has managed to grow its portfolio, which is why I see surprise potential for TRIN in 2026.
